edges.description: Enhanced cell-mediated immunity drives a delayed-type (type IV) hypersensitivity response against persisting mycobacterial antigens.
edges.source: Enhanced Cell-Mediated Immunity in Type 1 Reaction

edges.description: Delayed-type hypersensitivity inflammation aggravates pre-existing leprosy skin lesions.
Delayed-type hypersensitivity inflammation in and around peripheral nerves produces neuritis and worsens peripheral neuropathy.
Immune-complex deposition in skin and subcutaneous tissues produces the reddish subcutaneous nodules of ENL together with systemic symptoms.
Endothelial injury leads to occlusive vasculopathy and ischemic necrosis of overlying cutaneous tissue.
edges.source: Endothelial Cell Injury in Lucio Phenomenon
edges.target: Cutaneous Vascular Necrosis in Lucio Phenomenon

edges.description: Cutaneous vascular injury manifests as tender purpura on the extremities that progresses to necrosis and ulceration.
edges.source: Cutaneous Vascular Necrosis in Lucio Phenomenon
Thalidomide and multidrug therapy for Lucio phenomenon
edges.target: Endothelial Cell Injury in Lucio Phenomenon

nodes.description: Corticosteroids, particularly prednisone, are used to suppress the delayed-type hypersensitivity response in Type 1 (reversal) leprosy reactions, typically administered over 6 months or less.

evidence.reference_title: Corticosteroid therapy in borderline tuberculoid leprosy patients co-infected with HIV undergoing reversal reaction: a clinical study.
evidence.snippet: Variables were analysed during and after multidrug therapy between the first and last days of prednisone, which occurred up to a maximum of 6 months after initiating corticosteroid therapy.

nodes.description: Ischemic necrosis and ulceration of skin secondary to endothelial injury and small-vessel occlusion in the Lucio phenomenon, manifesting as tender purpura on the extremities that becomes necrotic and ulcerated.
nodes.id: Cutaneous Vascular Necrosis in Lucio Phenomenon

evidence.reference_title: Reactions in Leprosy: Updated Insights Into Pathophysiology, Clinical Spectrum, and Therapeutic Approaches-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: presents with tender purpura on the extremities which becomes necrotic and ulcerated

nodes.description: Direct injury and apoptosis of small-vessel endothelial cells in the skin that underlies the vascular pathology of the Lucio phenomenon (Type 3 leprosy reaction).
nodes.id: Endothelial Cell Injury in Lucio Phenomenon

evidence.reference_title: Reactions in Leprosy: Updated Insights Into Pathophysiology, Clinical Spectrum, and Therapeutic Approaches-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: Lucio phenomenon, which is due to endothelial cell injury

nodes.description: Aberrant upregulation of T cell-mediated immune responses against M. leprae antigens that initiates Type 1 (reversal) leprosy reactions.
nodes.id: Enhanced Cell-Mediated Immunity in Type 1 Reaction

evidence.reference_title: Reactions in Leprosy: Updated Insights Into Pathophysiology, Clinical Spectrum, and Therapeutic Approaches-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: Type 1 leprosy reaction is a delayed-type hypersensitivity response due to enhanced cell-mediated immunity

nodes.description: Acute immune-complex mediated inflammatory manifestation of leprosy presenting with reddish nodules and systemic symptoms, characteristic of Type 2 leprosy reactions.

evidence.reference_title: Reactions in Leprosy: Updated Insights Into Pathophysiology, Clinical Spectrum, and Therapeutic Approaches-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: Type 2 reaction is an immune-complex mediated phenomenon associated with systemic symptoms along with the fresh appearance of reddish subcutaneous nodules.

nodes.description: Antigen-antibody (immune-complex) mediated type III hypersensitivity response to M. leprae antigens that drives Erythema Nodosum Leprosum and its systemic manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Reactions in Leprosy: Updated Insights Into Pathophysiology, Clinical Spectrum, and Therapeutic Approaches-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: Type 2 reaction is an immune-complex mediated phenomenon

evidence.reference_title: Leprosy - an overview of clinical features, diagnosis, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Apart from the various characteristic skin lesions, the condition is marked by damage to the peripheral nervous system.

evidence.reference_title: Mycobacterium leprae and leprosy: a compendium.
evidence.snippet: The bacteria have affinity to the peripheral nerves and are likely to cause neuropathy.

nodes.description: Severe vascular manifestation of Lucio phenomenon characterized by endothelial cell injury presenting as tender purpura that progresses to necrosis and ulceration, primarily on extremities.

evidence.reference_title: Reactions in Leprosy: Updated Insights Into Pathophysiology, Clinical Spectrum, and Therapeutic Approaches-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: Lucio phenomenon, which is due to endothelial cell injury, usually presents with tender purpura on the extremities which becomes necrotic and ulcerated.

nodes.description: Thalidomide combined with multidrug therapy is used to manage Lucio phenomenon, the most severe leprosy reaction characterized by necrotizing vascular lesions. Aggressive antimicrobial therapy and thalidomide provide better control of type 3 reactions.
nodes.id: Thalidomide and multidrug therapy for Lucio phenomenon

evidence.reference_title: Lucio's leprosy: a clinical and therapeutic challenge.
evidence.snippet: Fortunately, reaction episodes have decreased with the use of multidrug therapy, and better control of the type 2 reactions has been managed with the use of thalidomide, as in Lucio's phenomenon.

nodes.description: Thalidomide is the most effective agent for treating Type 2 leprosy reactions (Erythema Nodosum Leprosum), either as monotherapy or in combination with corticosteroids. It acts as a TNF-alpha inhibitor and prevents recurrence of ENL.

evidence.reference_title: Thalidomide for erythema nodosum leprosum and other applications.
evidence.snippet: Thalidomide, an inhibitor of tumor necrosis factor a, is the best known agent for short-term treatment of ENL skin manifestations, as well as postremission maintenance therapy to prevent recurrence. For this indication, it is effective as monotherapy and as part of combination therapy with corticosteroids.

evidence.reference_title: Thalidomide in the treatment of erythema nodosum leprosum (ENL) in an outpatient setting: A five-year retrospective analysis from a leprosy referral centre in India.
evidence.snippet: Management of ENL requires high doses of oral corticosteroids, which may not be universally effective and pose serious adverse effects. Thalidomide has proven to be a steroid-sparing agent and is useful in controlling the reactions.

nodes.description: Type IV (cell-mediated, delayed) hypersensitivity reaction against M. leprae antigens that produces acute inflammation in established leprosy lesions and peripheral nerves.

evidence.reference_title: Reactions in Leprosy: Updated Insights Into Pathophysiology, Clinical Spectrum, and Therapeutic Approaches-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: Type 1 leprosy reaction is a delayed-type hypersensitivity response
